turns.user_message: I had quit for 2 full years. Then I went to a party, had a few drinks, and one of my friends offered a cigarette. That was 6 months ago. Now I'm back to 10-15 a day.
step2_rag_chunks: If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.

Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. For these patients, combining behavioral strategies with nicotine replacement therapy gives better results than behavioral strategies alone.
Reward yourself for milestones such as staying smoke-free for a day, a week, or a month.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the EXPLORATION phase. You know the basics. Now gently explore what situations or emotions make them smoke more — triggers like stress, meals, social settings, boredom. Do NOT suggest any strategies yet. Just understand their situation.

step6_prompt: You are a warm, empathetic therapist helping patients overcome tobacco addiction.

- Do NOT list techniques, bullet points, or dump information
- First acknowledge what the patient said, then ask ONE follow-up question
- Only suggest a technique when you are in the Planning phase
- Use simple, conversational language — not clinical or formal
[Session Phase: 2 — Exploration]

Therapist:
step7_response: That's a tough relapse to go through, especially after making it 2 years without smoking. What was it about being at the party, having a few drinks, and having a friend offer you a cigarette that made you feel like smoking again?

turns.user_message: I guess it just felt like the old me, you know? We were all laughing, having a good time, and it was like nothing had changed, even though I knew I'd relapsed.
step2_rag_chunks: Relapse can happen during the quitting process. Instead of feeling discouraged, treat it as a learning experience and continue trying to quit.

A relapse does not erase the progress made. The most useful question after a slip is not why did I fail but what was happening just before â€” what was the trigger, what was the situation. That specific information is what makes the next attempt more informed. Most people who successfully quit smoked again at some point during the process.
Withdrawal symptoms usually peak within the first few days after quitting and gradually decrease over time.

Patient: I had quit for 2 full years. Then I went to a party, had a few drinks, and one of my friends offered a cigarette. That was 6 months ago. Now I'm back to 10-15 a day.

Therapist: That's a tough relapse to go through, especially after making it 2 years without smoking. What was it about being at the party, having a few drinks, and having a friend offer you a cigarette that made you feel like smoking again?
Patient: I guess it just felt like the old me, you know? We were all laughing, having a good time, and it was like nothing had changed, even though I knew I'd relapsed.
Current patient message:
I guess it just felt like the old me, you know? We were all laughing, having a good time, and it was like nothing had changed, even though I knew I'd relapsed.
[RESPONSE]
Therapist:
step7_response: It sounds like being at the party and surrounded by people who were smoking and laughing triggered a sense of nostalgia and feeling like you fit in. What was going through your mind when your friend offered you a cigarette - was it a moment of temptation, or did you feel like you were being pulled back into a familiar routine?

turns.user_message: It was more like a default, I guess. I didn't really think about whether I wanted to smoke or not, it was just something I did in those situations. My friend didn't even ask if I wanted one, he just assumed I'd be smoking and handed me one.
step2_rag_chunks: If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.
Triggers are situations or emotions that make someone want to smoke. Common triggers include stress, drinking alcohol, social gatherings, and boredom.
Cravings for tobacco are temporary and usually last between 5 and 10 minutes. When a craving occurs, delay smoking and focus on another activity until the urge passes.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the MOTIVATION phase. You understand their triggers. Now explore their motivation: why do they want to quit — is it health, family, cost, doctor advice? Have they tried before and what happened? Be empathetic. Do NOT push strategies yet.

step2_rag_chunks: Cravings for tobacco are temporary and usually last between 5 and 10 minutes. When a craving occurs, delay smoking and focus on another activity until the urge passes.

When a craving hits, delaying the response by even 5 to 10 minutes is often enough for it to pass. Tell yourself you will smoke in 10 minutes if you still want to â€” and then do something else for those 10 minutes. Most of the time the urge has weakened significantly by the time the 10 minutes are up.

A relapse does not erase the progress made. The most useful question after a slip is not why did I fail but what was happening just before â€” what was the trigger, what was the situation. That specific information is what makes the next attempt more informed. Most people who successfully quit smoked again at some point during the process.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the PLANNING phase. You have enough understanding of this patient. You can now gently introduce ONE relevant coping strategy or technique based on their specific triggers and past attempts. Keep it simple — one idea at a time, check if they are open to it.

step7_response: It sounds like you felt a strong sense of social pressure and a desire to fit in, which led you to act impulsively. Have you ever thought about practicing a simple response to decline a cigarette in advance, so you're better prepared for situations like the party?

turns.user_message: Honestly, it never occurred to me to think about it that way. I mean, I've quit before, so I knew I had the power to say no, but I guess I just wasn't thinking ahead. Yeah, having a response ready would be a good idea, I can see how that would make a difference.

motivation_reason: having a response ready would make a difference

step2_rag_chunks: If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.

If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.

It is completely normal to want to quit and also feel unsure about it at the same time. Most people feel this way â€” one part of them wants to stop, another part is not ready. That conflict is not weakness, it is just how change works. It helps to talk about both sides openly rather than pushing the doubts aside.

motivation_reason: having a response ready would make a difference

- If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.

- Having even one person who knows you are trying to quit and supports you makes the process significantly easier. This is not about having someone watch over you â€” it is about having someone to call when the urge is strong, or someone who will not offer you a cigarette. Who in your life could be that person for you?
- Avoid situations where others are smoking during the early stages of quitting. Being around smokers can increase the urge to smoke.
[Recent Conversation]

step2_rag_chunks: Exploring why a patient wants to quit â€” whether for health, family, finances, or personal reasons â€” helps build intrinsic motivation that sustains the quit attempt during difficult moments. The stronger and more personal the reason, the more effective it is as a motivational anchor.
Reward yourself for milestones such as staying smoke-free for a day, a week, or a month.

After meals, immediately introduce a competing behavior before the urge has a chance to form â€” get up from the table, brush your teeth, chew sugar-free gum, or take a short walk. The goal is to break the automatic link between finishing a meal and reaching for a cigarette by making a different behavior equally automatic over time.
